text: her legs around his waist, drawing herself snugly against him . “There is only you .” His sincerity was clear. She pressed her lips to his. They moved as one, her hands exploring the altered landscape of his body. Only sensation remained; there was no room for words, no need . * * * R onin waited until Lara was sound asleep before he exited the bed. Despite his care, the springs and frame creaked with his movement . She stirred, lashes lifting, and peered at him with groggy eyes. Her fingers loosely closed around his forearm . “Need to go to the Clinic,” he said. “The sooner I’m repaired, the sooner we can leave .” “Okay.” Her eyelids fluttered shut. “I’ll be… waiting .” Her grip, already weak, slackened, and her hand slipped off his arm. It trailed heat in its wake. Gradually, her breathing evened out. Even now, knowing how important it was to repair the damage he’d sustained, it was difficult to leave her . Leaning down, he pulled on his boots, lacing them loosely. They’d be off again soon enough. He stood, easing his weight onto his right leg to test the strength of his knee. Diagnostics assessed its current state . Partial mobility restored. Actuator operating at 32% functionality, with 140% of normal power draw. Power leak probable . Though his pants were damaged, too, he pulled them up and fastened them. His steps were slow as he crossed the room to the closet. Each time he shifted his weight onto his right leg, his knee buckled, but the joint locks initiated before it collapsed. Not ideal, but he didn’t have far to go. Just another mile . He selected a clean pair of pants and found the shirt Lara had made for him, folding both articles of clothing together. He’d determine if his current pants were salvageable after he was repaired . Tucking the bundle under his arm, he walked back into the bedroom, optics dropping to the pistol atop the chest. Even within the Bot District, he never traveled unarmed. But what good would it do him ? Limping to the bedside, he stared down at Lara. Her breathing was soft, the beat of her heart steady and slow. She’d spoken of hearts and love, and what could he know about either? Humans didn’t often use the former term literally, and the latter … Careful not to lean on his damaged leg, he bent forward and pulled the blanket over Lara’s body. She slid her arms up, taking hold of the cloth, and wrapped it around herself snugly without opening her eyes. A long, satisfied sigh escaped her before she stilled . Love . Despite his caution, he descended the steps in half the time it had taken to mount them the night before. He went to the door and didn’t allow himself any further hesitation. He didn’t succumb to the desire to go look at her again, to contemplate the serenity on her face as she slept, to listen to the soothing sound of her breathing . His internal clock had meticulously accounted for every microsecond, every minute, hour, and day, since his reactivation. It was one of his core processes, albeit a relatively simple one. Something as natural to him as breathing was to a human. Every second had always been exactly as long as the ones preceding it and following it, without differentiation, without fail . Until Lara…until she changed everything . Every minute of his journey back to Cheyenne had felt longer than a year of his life before. Each step had been an impossible distance for his damaged stride. The possibility of never seeing Lara again had altered his perception of everything . Stepping outside, he closed and locked the door behind him. The merest hint of dawn left the sky a dark, smudgy gray. He walked north, toward Warlord’s pieced- together wall . Love . He understood the word, at its most basic level. It was a deep attachment to someone. How could a seemingly simple term carry so much weight, so much depth ? To his left, leaves rustled in the persistent wind, a reminder that life always found a way — even if it needed help from time to time . Curiosity had taken root in him, that first night, and it had sprouted into fascination. Now, it was a tree tall enough to put all others in its shade. Lara was part of his existence. The simulations he ran regarding his future invariably included her, and he found himself dismissing any that didn’t — because he knew he wouldn’t be able to let her go . Despite the loathing she’d viewed him with in the beginning, Lara saw him . Not a case housing a collection of parts, not a bot…just Ronin. The change in her was clear, highlighted by her struggles against it . She’d told him she loved him. That was no small admission, those weren’t careless words of comfort. They both knew words could be cheap and meaningless, that they could be used without conviction, spoken with duplicity or outright malice. But she had proved her words through her actions . Though he couldn’t be sure — this was all too new to him, too difficult to sort out in a mind operating on logic and mathematics — he thought he loved her , too . Ronin turned the corner and proceeded east, parallel to the wall across the street. The man who’d written the attic journal had undoubtedly walked this same road, years before. The writer had loved his family, had undoubtedly been loved by them, and had lived before Warlord tore their already damaged world apart . Despite the powerful storms, the lack of resources, and the sharp contrasts of heat and cold, the Dust wasn’t the true danger. It was a force of nature, incapable of malice. People like Warlord were what Lara needed to be protected from.
